Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study maybe an hour here or there when I felt like it, but nothing consistent. With limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could actually focus, which wasn't often. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't seem to make myself care enough to stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd sometimes go days without touching anything, then suddenly panic when a deadline was coming up. I tried to set regular times, but life kept getting in the way—work, anxiety, just feeling overwhelmed. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or just pure guilt that I hadn't logged in for a while. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about something due and that would finally push me to check in. I rarely logged in just because I was curious or excited about the material, if I'm being honest.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every morning with my coffee. But that fell apart pretty quickly when my anxiety spiked and everything felt too overwhelming. The LMS never became something I naturally reached for.
Q5: The videos were actually helpful when I could focus on them—I watched a lot of those. As a kinesthetic learner, I needed something more engaging than just reading. The discussion posts were okay too because seeing other people's thoughts sometimes helped things click for me.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the extra resources they posted. PDFs and long articles just don't work for my brain—I need to be doing something, not just reading. I'd open them, skim for a few seconds, then close them and tell myself I'd come back later. I rarely did.
Q7: If I could see an immediate payoff—like it counting toward my grade or helping me understand something I was stuck on—then it felt worth doing. Activities that seemed disconnected from what I actually needed to pass the course felt like a waste of time. I needed to feel like my effort was going somewhere.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, my brain just categorized it as 'not important' even when I knew it might help. Required activities at least had the threat of consequences, which was sometimes the only thing that got me to do them.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the videos and some materials to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd watch things on my phone during breaks at work or while commuting. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded videos, and none of that showed up anywhere. I also did a lot of just thinking through problems while doing other things—like at work or before bed. That kind of processing doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video or PDF once, then use it multiple times offline over the next few weeks. My login would show I accessed it once, but I probably reviewed it three or four times on my own. The LMS just can't track that kind of repeated offline use.
Q12: When my anxiety got really bad, everything felt impossible. There were weeks where just opening the LMS made my chest tight. I'd stare at the screen and feel completely paralyzed, like I couldn't even start. The mindfulness exercises they recommended helped a little, but not enough to really turn things around.
Q13: Absolutely. I work to support myself, so when shifts got busy, studying was the first thing to go. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just scroll without really absorbing anything. The fatigue made everything feel like it took twice as much effort.
Q14: All the time. I'd have the course open while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about money or just feeling anxious about everything. I'd watch videos without really processing them, or click through readings without any of it sticking. My body was there but my brain wasn't.
Q15: I avoided the assignments that felt too hard or confusing. My anxiety would convince me that I'd fail anyway, so why bother starting? I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It was this awful cycle of avoidance that I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only structure I had. I'd do almost nothing until a few days before something was due, then scramble to get it done. It wasn't a good system—my work suffered because of it—but without that pressure, I just couldn't motivate myself to engage.
Q17: When I got feedback that was critical, it honestly made me want to engage less. It confirmed my fears that I wasn't good enough. Positive feedback helped a little, but by the time I got it, I'd usually already moved on mentally. The feedback loop felt too slow to really change my behavior.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with four online courses at once. I couldn't keep track of what was due when across all of them. Having everything online meant there was no external structure forcing me to show up, which was bad for someone like me who needs that accountability.
Q19: The flexibility was supposed to be a benefit, but for me it made things harder. I need more hands-on, structured activities to stay engaged. The course design assumed a level of self-discipline that I just don't have right now. More interactive elements or check-ins might have helped me stay on track.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in, click around, watch parts of videos, start things without finishing them. It looks like activity but it's really just anxious scrolling or trying to figure out what I should be doing. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone is really efficient or studies mostly offline like I did sometimes. They might download everything once, master it on their own, and just show up for assessments. That wasn't quite my experience since I still struggled with exams, but I get how it's possible.
Q22: The log can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but it can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just staring blankly at the screen. It misses all the offline work, the mental processing, and definitely can't capture how anxious or overwhelmed you were while engaging.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just doing the work—it's wanting to do it, or at least feeling like it matters. I attended almost every session and did most tasks, but I rarely felt truly engaged in that deeper sense.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much my anxiety controlled everything I did in this course. It can't show the hours I spent worrying instead of working, or how hard it was just to open my laptop some days. It also misses that I was genuinely trying, even when my results didn't show it.
